summary: Mitchell Hashimoto provides an update on Ghostty's development, highlighting major new features including a terminal inspector (similar to browser dev tools), improved Asian language input support, custom shader capabilities, and an xterm compatibility audit. The beta testing group has grown from 100 to over 350 people, with stability improving dramatically—beta waves that once caused dozens of bug reports now handle 50+ new testers smoothly. The post emphasizes Ghostty's commitment to being a proper platform for terminal applications while maintaining high performance and compatibility standards.
key_points: The terminal inspector brings web-inspector-style debugging to terminal development, showing live keyboard input, terminal modes, cell metadata, and more—aimed at making terminal app development easier across all terminals
Asian language input (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) now works well after inviting testers from diverse timezones revealed nearly a dozen issues with IME handling and character rendering
Custom shaders allow CRT effects and accessibility customizations while using minimal resources (~1% CPU, ~2% GPU), leveraging Shadertoy's interface for easy shader development
Ghostty now follows xterm as the gold standard for terminal behavior, providing consistent answers to 'why does this feature behave this way?' questions
Nearly 1 in 6 beta testers contributes code, with almost 50 contributors and over 100 bug fixes in two months
money_quotes: A terminal is an application platform. I think the word 'platform' is thrown around way too much by people trying to believe their work is more important than it actually is.
A terminal is not interesting without the applications that run in it. Going further, a terminal is pointless without applications that run in it.
One way of increasing the quantity and quality of terminal applications is to make it easier to build for the terminal.
The general inefficiency of most modern software has taken us all for fools! CPUs and GPUs are super fast and this is barely doing anything.
It's been so long since this hardware has been readily available or practically used that most modern terminal emulators just kind of mimic each others behavior.
The primary practical use case for custom shaders is accessibility.
I'm not looking for investors. I hope no one interprets this paragraph in that way, but I feel I have to say this.
key_insight: Building a great terminal emulator requires treating it as a genuine application platform, which means investing in developer tooling, internationalization, and rigorous compatibility testing rather than just adding flashy features.
